content.full_text:
MASSACHUSETTS FINANCIAL SERVICES COMPANY
500 BOYLSTON STREET · BOSTON · MASSACHUSETTS 02116
617 · 954-5000

A. KEITH BRODKIN
President

March 14, 1990

Ms. Kathryn Donovan
General Counsel
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 600
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Donovan:

Disclosure Requirements - Form 721

I am writing to you with regard to disclosure requirements as outlined in Form 721. Massachusetts Financial Services (MFS) is an investment manager for the Los Angeles County Employees' Retirement Association (LACERA) and is responsible for managing a domestic fixed-income portfolio.

As a result of your February 27 memorandum to "Interested Persons" it is our understanding that the Fair Political Practices Commission is currently developing regulations to define the scope of the phrase "other public officials who manage public investments" to determine who is subject to the conflict-of-interest disclosure requirements of the Political Reform Act. We understand that you believe that the term should be narrowly construed and that it would not include "employees of investment firms." We agree.

We would strongly protest any requirement that individual members of this organization be required to disclose personal income and assets, and we urge that the Commissions' final position confirm that employees are not subject to the disclosure provisions of the Act for the following reasons:

1. MFS has a corporate relationship with LACERA. The arrangement is not between individuals. Furthermore, those individuals to whom MFS has delegated responsibility for management of LACERA's assets may be, and have sometimes been, reassigned by MFS without approval of LACERA. There is thus no independent relationship between individuals at MFS and LACERA.

2. MFS is a registered investment advisor under the Investment Advisors Act of 1940 and is thus subject to the Code of Ethics established thereunder. Accordingly, MFS requires disclosure of all security transactions from its personnel to prevent conflict with client accounts.

Ms. Kathryn Donovan  
March 14, 1990

3. MFS receives a fee based on size of assets managed, not on transactions. Therefore, no individual can benefit from any type of account activity.

4. Fees paid by LACERA amounted to only 0.17% (17/100 of 1%) of MFS's gross revenues in 1989. No revenue was generated by any transaction.

5. MFS is required by LACERA to report annually any improper conduct or transactions between its personnel and personnel of LACERA.

6. MFS, in the course of carrying out its responsibilities, must report all securities transactions for LACERA's account within 24 hours. Complete monthly reports of all activities are provided, and regular meetings are held at which LACERA personnel review reports and question MFS portfolio managers about their activities. All meetings are open to the public and are customarily covered by the press.

7. Salary data is confidential within MFS and public disclosure to competitors would be unfair.

For these reasons, we consider it inappropriate and unwarranted for individuals employed by an investment management organization which is retained by LACERA to be required to disclose publicly their income and assets to the California Fair Political Practices Commission.

Very truly yours,

A. Keith Brodkin

AKB:DMH:chn

cc: Ms. Diane Sandoval, LACERA
